> Remove entire query does not suffice for my needs.
> 
> In general, I just need to replace the query when the key exists, to
> add the query when the key not exists.
>
> Because if I directly add the query, sometimes the query key will be
> multi.

`furl.query.set()` performs exactly the actions you describe. If the
query key already exists, `furl.query.set()` replaces it (even
multivalue keys, like `?one=a&one=b`).

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/?a=a&b=1&b=2')
>>> f.query.set({'b':'REPLACED'})
>>> f.url
'http://www.google.com/?a=a&b=REPLACED'
```

And if the query key doesn't exist, `furl.query.set()` adds it.

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/?a=a')
>>> f.query.set({'b':'ADDED'})
>>> f.url
'http://www.google.com/?a=a&b=ADDED'
```

So, to back up a bit and make sure we're on the same page:
`furl.query.set()` does exactly what you want -- you'd just like a
similar method on the `furl` object, too, not just the `Query` object,
right? Something like

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/?a=a&b=b')
>>> f.set_query({'b':'REPLACED'})
>>> f.url
'http://www.google.com/?a=a&b=REPLACED'
```

Do I understand you correctly?

Thanks for doing this. I don't have time to look at the actual spyglass implementation right now, but I'll answer your general points

Yes, I think you're right. It's time to give up the distinction between tools. There is already very little code that differs between the build and sim backends. I think the timing is right too, as I'm hoping to finish up CAPI2 and this will have an impact on the decisions there. We should probably bike-shed the actual implementation details a bit, but I think a `fusesoc run` command would be good, with `--setup`, `--build` and `--launch` to run any of the three stages. (Note. No flags would run them all. launch corresponds to pgm for "build" backends). I think in that case that we can't auto-detect which tool to use (which is probably ok actually) and have to pass it on the command-line, like `fusesoc <fusesoc options> run <general run-options> vivado <vivado_options> supersoc <soc_options>`. Actually... that's quite good as it would give us a natural place to add tool-specific extra-options. I like that. We need to think a bit about how to handle the `--target` parameter though (what used to be `--testbench` for simulation flows, but will support different synthesis/P&R targets as well with CAPI2), but it should be doable

A small note regarding caching results. FuseSoC used to do this by default up until FuseSoC 1.2, which meant that users had to run with --force almost every time to avoid reusing old files. I planned to do some smart caching here to only update if the sources changed, but never got around to do that, so I added the --keep argument instead to let users decide

Agree that handling more advanced pipelines is better with shell scripts

> Can you tell me why you think it's important to have it in the library?

I am working on a command line tool that can merge adressbook data from different sources (https://github.com/xi/cctool). This is mostly for my own use by I also try to make it useful for others. 

One of the source formats should be LDIF-CONTENT because that is what you can export from thunderbird.

I started with [https://github.com/rbarrois/python-ldap/blob/master/Lib/ldif.py](the ldif module from python-ldap). But that does not have python3 support. It also failed parsing the data exported from thunderbird which I later found was actually a [thunderbird bug](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=230334).

I searched for a LDIF library which supports python3 and came across yours. When you told me that what I wanted was out of the scope of this project I went one creating my own fork of the python-ldap module at https://github.com/xi/ldif3.

That is of course far from ideal because I don't really have the time or the knowledge to maintain a high quality LIDF library. On the bright side I can implement a "less strict mode"  in my own library that works around existing thunderbird bugs.

> A link to possible ldif parsers would be helpful, but I do not really know of any I could recommend.

I guess I just hoped that there is a great LDIF library somewhere and I just failed to find it.

> Regarding the parsing of LDIF It could definitively be done for the LDIF-CHANGE format (that reflects atomic ldap operations) but not for LDIF-CONTENT, because of forward references (in this case you absolutely need a tool from the specific ldap server).

I am not completely sure but I guess you are refering to the cases where the attribute value is a URL. The [spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2849) explicitly states that most protocols are optional:

      6) When a URL is specified in an attrval-spec, the following
          conventions apply:

         a) Implementations SHOULD support the file:// URL format.  The
            contents of the referenced file are to be included verbatim
            in the interpreted output of the LDIF file.
         b) Implementations MAY support other URL formats.  The
            semantics associated with each supported URL will be
            documented in an associated Applicability Statement.

The python-ldap module mentioned above simply uses `urllib` for this task.

Active directory has a specific filter for nested groupmembership:

```
(&(objectClass=user)(memberof:1.2.840.113556.1.4.1941:=%s)) 
```
just replace ``%s`` with your root group dn.

Your code should be similar to this:

```
from ldap3 import Server, Connection, ALL, AUTO_BIND_TLS_BEFORE_BIND, SUBTREE

AD_HOST='my_server'
AD_USER_DOMAIN = 'my_domain'
AD_USER = 'my_user'
AD_PASS = 'my_password'
AD_BASE = 'my_base'
GROUP_DN = 'my_group'

ad_server = Server(
    AD_HOST,
    use_ssl=True,
    get_info=ALL
)

connection = Connection(
    ad_server,
    auto_bind=AUTO_BIND_TLS_BEFORE_BIND,
    read_only=True,
    check_names=True,
    user='%s\%s' % (AD_USER_DOMAIN, AD_USER),
    password=AD_PASS,
    return_empty_attributes=True
)

def get_group_users(cn):
    with connection as conn:
        conn.search(
            search_base=AD_BASE,
            search_filter='(&(objectClass=user)(memberof:1.2.840.113556.1.4.1941:=' + cn +'))',
            search_scope=SUBTREE,
            attributes = ['objectClass', 'userAccountControl'])
        members = []
        for entry in conn.entries:
            if not entry.userAccountControl in [546, 514, 66050, 66082]:
                members.append(entry.entry_dn)

        print(members)
        print(len(members))

get_group_users(GROUP_DN)
```

You have all the active members in the member list. Let me know if this works for you.

Bye,
Giovanni

This issue sometimes happens to me. First make sure that `bluetooth.service` is running. You can also try these command on `bluetoothctl`:

```
scan on
remove xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx (try to remove the misbehaving old pairing)
trust xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx
pair xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx
scan off (turning off scanning may help, or it might just be for the worse)
connect xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx (you can try to manually connect the controller after pairing)
```
where `xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx` is the MAC address of your controller. For each step you can try to put your controller to the pairing mode (hold PS+SHARE) or the normal mode (tap PS).

I ran all the unit tests and examples from the face_recognition lib and everything worked perfectly. So surprisingly, I wasn't able to find any issues at all. However I didn't test every function of dlib, just those relating to face stuff.

Also, the computer I have available at the moment doesn't have an nvidia card, so I only tested non-CUDA builds on Mac (2.9 GHz Intel Core i7):

```
python3 setup.py install --yes USE_AVX_INSTRUCTIONS --no DLIB_USE_CUDA
```

I also wanted to verify that there weren't any major changes to performance, so I ran [a very simple benchmark](https://github.com/ageitgey/face_recognition/blob/master/examples/benchmark.py). Here are the results across dlib versions:

|                                    | dlib 19.7            | dlib 19.8.99 master  | dlib 19.8.99 no-boost |
|------------------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|-----------------------|
| Face locations (240p)              | 0.0554s (18.05 fps)  | 0.0560s (17.86 fps)  | 0.0553s (18.08 fps)   |
| Face landmarks (240p)              | 0.0022s (455.91 fps) | 0.0023s (436.39 fps) | 0.0024s (416.37 fps)  |

Hi, thank you for reporting the problem. This is a known issue. Currently only the node.js install of durable_rules is supported in Windows. durable_rules relies on hiredis, which is available in Windows compiled with the VS 10.0 or VS 14.0 toolsets. Unfortunately Python 2.7 still relies on the VS 9.0 toolset, which has a different set of header files.

We have two options at this point:
1. Fork hiredis to compile using VS 9.0 toolset
2. Implement support for Python 3.x in all operating systems

Option #1 is not my favorite as it would be very difficult to keep up to date.  #2 seems like a good investment as durable_rules would be available to all Python 3.x users.

What do you think?

Hey, a couple options:

1) A boolean union of the two primitives. These can be flakey (try `engine='scad'`, it is quite a bit more robust than blender), but then you'd get one watertight mesh without internal geometry if it went right and can be sampled normally. 
2) Sample both independently, and then use KDTree queries to remove points from the second set that are within some radius of the first set. This is probably going to be the fastest and most reliable option, but will only really make sense in the "touching" case, and not do anything in instances where there is penetration.
3) Sample independently and then use `mesh.nearest.signed_distance` to remove points inside the  other mesh.
4) If mesh faces were actually exactly the same (which two cubes touching would not be, due to winding crossing the triangles) you could concatenate then then call `remove_duplicate_faces`

Best,
Mike

Hey, I think you may want to use `mesh.bounding_box` which is the axis aligned bounding box rather than `mesh.bounding_box_oriented` which is the oriented bounding box. Although in order to get the 8 corners of the OBB you could do:
`trimesh.bounds.corners(m.bounding_box_oriented.bounds)`

The mesh.bounds property is computed and always axis- aligned, where the .primitive.extents is pre-transform (by .primitive.transform)

One thing you might consider, is that the oriented bounding box you're using will probably have a lot less volume. You could generate the grid (axis aligned) the size of the OBB extents, and then transform the grid of query points using the OBB's transform.

AAA authorization and automation do not go very well together. The TACACS+ server is unable (generally to keep up with the requirements of automation). It will be even more of a problem if you start trying to do things concurrently (to multiple devices).

You could try adding a global_delay_factor to your ConnectHandler call. Start at 2, then 4, then 8 and keep increasing it upwards. This will slow Netmiko down (i.e. increase the amount of sleep time).

You could also with send_command use an expect_string argument and specify the pattern to use (to indicate that the command is done). This will cause Netmiko to stop sending the newlines to discover the prompt (in send_command method).

Being able to visualize 1, 2, or N annotations together would be my preference, though it might not fit into the ref/est paradigm of mir_eval.

and

> That, I think, is a separate issue. In JAMS, I'd be happy to just stack up the entire list of annotations vertically, since they'll generally be heterogeneous anyway.
> Ref-vs-est would be a separate function (functions?) on top of the core plotting routines that make head-to-head comparisons easier.

This makes sense for JAMS and not `mir_eval`, I think.  For example, `sonify` doesn't provide mechanisms for sonifying multiple things and combining them together (in different channels? on top of each other? etc) and I am comfortable leaving this up to the end-user (or `mir_eval`-using library, like JAMS).  Giving axis-in, axis-out access provides the most generic, straightforward, and easy-to-use flexibility for this.  If anyone can come up with a clean and flexible way to do something which is otherwise hard for the end-user to do (i.e. more than a few lines of code), I'm happy to consider it, but I'm also very comfortable with `mir_eval` not trying to handle this stuff.

For the licensing concerns, I am also unclear about the licensing on this project too which could be a bigger concern. From the code perspective, it's clear. Having MIT license is perfect to be freely open source (I assume that it is the goal of this project to have freely open source library that anyone can use for any purpose). The source code part is fine and we can keep the it as MIT license.

From the data perspective, because the model's weights are trained from BEST corpus, NECTEC could later come and argue that the weights are derived works of BEST which should be bound to the same CC-BY-NC-SA license requirements of BEST. That means, the data (and arguably the weights) is prohibited to be used in commercial settings due to the "NC" condition that NECTEC imposed in their license. That would be counter to the goal of freely open source of this project. 

In term of what kind of licensing that is widely accepted for this kind of open source projects, I would say CC-BY-SA is the common denominator as acceptable for all for data licensing. The same license is also used by Wikipedia. So if we use Thai Wikipedia as a corpus, then our model might be bound to the CC-BY-SA anyway. 
 
This means, if we want to do it right, the license for the current best_cnn3.h5 might need to be CC-BY-NC-SA and provide attribution to BEST from NECTEC.

We may need to create another set of of weights that are not trained from BEST for us to be able to license the weights under CC-BY-SA. We can use other free method of tokenization (like dictionary based) with lower accuracy to generate a small seed of corpus from Thai Wikipedia and use for training. I think we can still test our pre-trained model with BEST corpus to know the accuracy based on the BEST benchmark without having to worry about their license as we wouldn't use that as part of the training (we just use to check our model result). We can improve the accuracy further by acquiring more CC-BY-SA corpora.

There's no easy solutions here, as the general assumption is that a partition without a leader indicates a cluster in the middle of failover, which typically is a fairly transient thing, so retries make sense. I would not immediately throw an error because that would require every service to have to be aware enough of Kafka internals to know to handle this error, which sounds error prone and tedious. I also would prefer to keep the default timeout at infinite, because if I have a partition that has a few hours of downtime, I don't want to also have to worry about tracking down all consumers to restart them once that partition is fixed.

That said, I do see how it'd be nice to be able to override the infinite timeout.

As for how Java handles it, a brief overview:
* It introduces a new ConnectionState - CHECKING_API_VERSIONS. It will send if after SSL handshake, or after connect of unencrypted connections. See https://github.com/apache/kafka/blob/342f34a1994caafa3cea94a2f85ffd219b8cebc5/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/NetworkClient.java#L715
* It checks if request is supported exactly before sending request: https://github.com/apache/kafka/blob/342f34a1994caafa3cea94a2f85ffd219b8cebc5/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/NetworkClient.java#L353
* Instead of preparing an exact protocol version it uses a Builder class that can choose the best one to send.

Benchmark results with:

```python
# mkdirs.py
from os import mkdir
from os.path import join

def mkdirs(parent, depth=0):
    if depth == 5:
        for f in range(10):
            with open(join(parent, str(f)), 'w'):
                pass
        return
        
    depth += 1
    
    for d in range(10):
        newdir = join(parent, str(d))
        mkdir(newdir)
        mkdirs(newdir, depth)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    mkdirs('/storage/test')
```

That's 111111 directories (and 1 million files). Testing with:

```python
# test.py
from inotify.adapters import InotifyTree

if __name__ == '__main__':
    i = InotifyTree('/storage/test')
```

This is happening on a rotational drive. The benchmark (I did several runs and chose the best result):

```
$ sudo sysctl fs.inotify.max_user_watches=1000000 # make sure we don't hit file watch limit
fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 1000000
$ sudo sysctl vm.vfs_cache_pressure=0 # never auto-clear vfs cache
vm.vfs_cache_pressure = 0
$ echo 2 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches # drop dentry cache
2

$ time python3 test.py # python3 test *without* dentry cache

real	0m19.287s
user	0m2.395s
sys	0m6.483s

$ time python3 test.py # python3 test *with* dentry cache

real	0m18.905s
user	0m2.587s
sys	0m6.155s

$ echo 2 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches
2

$ time python2 test.py # python2 test *without* dentry cache

real	1m17.637s
user	0m4.165s
sys	0m26.825s

$ time python2 test.py # python2 test *with* dentry cache

real	1m16.991s
user	0m4.376s
sys	0m25.833s
```

So that's about 1/4th of the time.
Interestingly enough dentry cache doesn't make much of a difference (or my test procedure was wrong - I can oly guess it's not hitting the cache despite the 0 vfs_cache_pressure).

Just out of curiosity, I tried old code for comparison (without flushing cache):

```
$ git checkout HEAD^
[...]
HEAD is now at 78670d8 Version bump.

$ time python3 test.py

real	1m17.761s
user	0m6.275s
sys	0m24.890s

$ time python2 test.py

real	1m16.418s
user	0m4.408s
sys	0m25.065s
```

So, a negligible difference in the os.listdir() loop vs os.walk(), when os.scandir() is not involved.

As i mentioned, this was on an HDD. On an SSD the results are:

```
new code, yes cache:
python 3: real	0m25.734s
python 2: real	0m45.174s

old code, yes cache:
python 3: real	0m52.341s
python 2: real	0m45.708s
```
Not so dramatic, but still a serious improvement.

```python
        # user = helper.register_new_user(username, email)  # this would return
                                                            # a user object or
                                                            # dict, like bellow
        user = {"user_id": 20}

        access_token, output = await self.responses.get_access_token_output(
            request,
            user,
            self.config,
            self.instance)

        # no refresh token configured, so, this won't work at all
        # refresh_token = await self.instance.auth.get_refresh_token(request, user)
        # output.update({
        #     self.config.refresh_token_name: refresh_token
        # })

        response = self.responses.get_token_reponse(
            request,
            access_token,
            output,
            # refresh_token=refresh_token,  # no refresh token
            config=self.config)

        return response


my_views = (
    ('/register', Register),
)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    app = Sanic(__name__)
    Initialize(
        app,
        class_views=my_views,
        authenticate=lambda: True,
        refresh_token=False
    )
    app.run()
```

````python
        self.funcfilter = self.p.funcfilter
        self.dataname = self.p.dataname

    def _load(self):
        # Tell underlying source to get next data
        while self.dataname.next():
            # Try to load the data from the underlying source
            if self._load_data():
                return True  # loaded ... say so

            # else go back to the beginning of the loop

        return False  # no more data from underlying source

    def _load_data(self):
        if not self.funcfilter(self.dataname):
            return False  # funcfilter doesn't like the data

        # Data is allowed - Copy and confirm with return
        for i in self.dataname.size():  # size is "number of lines"
            self.lines[i][0] = self.dataname.lines[i][0]

        return True
````

As a side note I did figure out what to do to get around this...

The application is a simple CherryPy/Celery REST style micro service. I organized the application into a single module which is where this all started...

```
setup.py
package/
    __init__.py
    __main__.py
    rest/...
    tasks/...
```

To get the entry_point working to start up the CherryPy service I put `main()` in `__init__.py`. That worked for a time, then the update came out and resulted in this stack dump. Celery was walking the `package` module as well as the `tasks` module. I run the Celery tasks as `python -m celery -A package.tasks`. So I moved the relevant imports and main method to the `__main__.py` (which is probably where it should be anyway). This cleaned up the project a bit anyway as CherryPy and Celery services don't import the high level classes but can share common classes and methods.

I found it odd that Celery is doing magic to overload the import of the `tasks` module. My guess is that if `package` was a namespace instead of a module it would not work, not tested that though.

Just my 2 cents for how I worked around the issue.

The real implementation that supports parallelization would be a bit more complex (but certainly doable). Through the context, a `context.accumulator()` call would create an instance of `Accumulator` but also register it in an accumulator-registry. Those instances would have to be serialized out to workers in a map operation along with everything else and merged back on the driver when done.

I guess those would be useful for collecting data about the distributed operations. We already collect some timing information in `context._stats`, but Accumulators could be used to do this a bit nicer and expose an interface to the user to add to that.

Currently the avatar field provided by django-machina is only associated with the forum profile of a user (and not directly to the user itself). The forum profile, which corresponds to the `ForumProfile` model (see [here](https://github.com/ellmetha/django-machina/blob/master/machina/apps/forum_member/abstract_models.py#L17)), is necessary because it embeds some useful values such as posts counts for each user. Sure thing, there is an `avatar` field on this model allowing to set avatars associated with each forum profile, but in real world applications avatars will be generally associated with the `User` model directly. So the `avatar` field provided by the `ForumProfile` is just here to provide basic support for avatars when you're quickly setting up a forum instance.

That said, it is true that the common use case generally involves using an avatar associated with your `User` model directly (eg. with django-avatar). If you're doing this while using django-machina, your only choice will be to override all the templates (and probably some views) displaying the forum users' avatars in order to ensure that the forum pages use the right avatar field.

Perhaps adding support for django-avatar is not necessarily the best thing to do. Instead, we could perhaps add new generic settings and helpers allowing to define where and how avatars should be retrieved / displayed (plus some documentation). Currently django-machina doesn't provide contrib modules (like Django does), but it could also be a solution to add better support for solutions like django-avatar (if applicable). What do you think?

Django uses the __str__ method of models all over the place, most notably
in the admin. The standard Django admin history view uses the stored
__str__ of the object in an object_repr field on the AdminAction model.
Django-reversion does the same thing on the Version model to build the
enhanced history view that replaces the built-in one.

Django doesn't use __repr__ anywhere, and uses __str__ to refer to and
describe individual model instances, and thus so does reversion.

__repr__ is only meant to create a string that is for programmer reference,
and is ideally also a valid Python expression. It's not for end users. A
good __repr__ for a model might be 'YourModel(name="foo"), and the
equivalent __str__ would just be '"foo"'. The first is for programmers, the
second is for users.

Hello,

Sorry, for some reason I didn't notice this report/question.
I'm not sure what you are meaning by "fuzzer": Trying to pump random data to a USB device to test it, or a fuzzer for something else which just happens to be controlled over USB ?
For the former, I guess USB is complex enough that just random pokes would be inefficient. Devices may not produce any data on an endpoint until configured enough on another endpoint.
As to the async API, I think you should stick with sync API until you have something working, and only then think about performance (which is just what async API is about, not really functionality (well, isochronous transfers are only available via async API, but precisely because of latency constraints AFAIK).

As for a working example with >35MB/s performance using async API (but not overly complicated), there is my [userland driver for the International Test Instruments 1480A USB device](https://github.com/vpelletier/ITI1480A-linux/blob/master/iti1480a/capture.py#L416) (which happens to be a USB protocol analyser, but you shouldn't need to worry about this to understand the code).

Basic principle of async API usage:
- allocate several transfers (enough so that USB host controller has several transfers to fill while python code is handling transfers which completed)
- tell each transfer what to run on completion
- submit all transfers, and resubmit same transfer on (successful) completion
- have something (thread, your program's main polling loop, ...) call into libusb when needed: this is what will actually execute transfer completion code. You can use USBPollerThread class for ease of use: instantiate, call start(), and forget it.

Storing the password ended up being a **Bad Idea**, and it was removed a while back. There's a very real chance that an admin could enter their actual password into the honeypot login, which would display it plainly to anyone else with access to the admin or database. **This is a Very Bad Thing**, since the only way to remove it is to delete it at the database level. This exact scenario has happened to myself and others.

It doesn't really matter what password was entered, just that someone was potentially maliciously trying to access the admin. The information that *is* collected -- username, session key, user agent, and IP address -- should be enough.

Please don't store the password.

````python
The BaseModel is just a simple model to track created/modified times for objects.
BaseAdmin sets a few features I consider 'nice' in django-admin.

#models.py
```
class BaseModel(models.Model):                                                                                              
                                                                                                                            
    """ BaseModel provides information on creation and modification times for all child objects. """                        
                                                                                                                            
    created = models.DateTimeField(editable=False, blank=True, null=True)                                                   
    modified = models.DateTimeField(editable=False, blank=True, null=True)                                                  
                                                                                                                            
    def save(self, *args, **kwargs):                                                                                        
        """ Save an object and note the created or modified time. """                                                       
        if not self.pk:                                                                                                     
            self.created = timezone.now()                                                                                   
        self.modified = timezone.now()                                                                                      
        super(BaseModel, self).save(*args, **kwargs)                                                                        
                                                                                                                            
    class Meta:                                                                                                             
        abstract = True                                                                                                     
```

#admin.py
```
class BaseAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):

    """ Base module inherited by all other admin modules to save time setting various defaults. """

    actions_on_top = True
    actions_on_bottom = True
    save_as = True
    save_on_top = True

```

So a super hacky way I was able to get around this was to hijack the URL in the OAuth2Server object of the gather_keys_oauth2.py script. Initialize the object:
```server = OAuth2.OAuth2Server(client_id, consumer_secret)```
Then just pull out the URL directly:
```auth_url = server.oauth.authorize_token_url(redirect_uri = server.redirect_uri)[0]```
Then you can mangle the URL with whatever parameters (such as prompt) you'd like.
```` 

The problem is due to in CDH5.9 it is CentOS 7, but in CDH 5.8 it is 6.7.
I solved the problem by reinstalling the **_'sasl'_** library in a CentOS 7 VM before uploading the whole python environment to the CDH cluster:
`sudo yum install cyrus-sasl-devel` (required for compiling during pip install)
`cd <miniconda home>/bin`
`./pip install impyla==0.14.0`
`./pip install thrift_sasl==0.2.1` (It will install the dependent **sasl 0.2.1**)

The compiled **sasl** python module will then import the new libsasl2 instead of the old one.

For the issue #1, I was thinking the case when a replaced value have an another kind of marker string that is not processed yet.  For example, when a sql text is something like "inert into table (time_col) values (?)" and the argument is a time string such as "2015-11-26 11:11:11", the original code first replaces "?" to "2015-11-26 11:11:11" by the first call of "operation = replace_markers('?', operation, string_parameters)" in the _replace_numeric_markers() function. The resulting value of the operation variable will be "inert into table (time_col) values (2015-11-26 11:11:11)".  Since the variable includes ":1" string, this is considered as a numeric marker and replaced again at the end of the process in the _replace_numeric_markers() function.

The similar thing happens when using a "%s" marker, and replaced value includes a time string, also in the case when using a "?" marker, and replaced string include "%s" string.

The zmq FD attribute shouldn't be used with pollers, and the FD attribute of a socket will *not* behave as you would expect, since it's an edge-triggered, read-only FD, not a level-triggered read-write FD. If you are encountering this with tornado, it's because you haven't told tornado to use the ZMQPoller, which you must do for it to work properly:

```python
from zmq.eventloop import ioloop
ioloop.install()
```

sudo pip uninstall deepspeech
make clean
make setup.cfg
python ./setup.py bdist_wheel

found the issue, the signature is not parsing correctly because of this bit of code in class SignatureV4 (packet/packets.py):

```python
    @pubalg.register(int)
    @pubalg.register(PubKeyAlgorithm)
    def pubalg_int(self, val):
        self._pubalg = PubKeyAlgorithm(val)

        sigs = {PubKeyAlgorithm.RSAEncryptOrSign: RSASignature,
                PubKeyAlgorithm.RSAEncrypt: RSASignature,
                PubKeyAlgorithm.RSASign: RSASignature,
                PubKeyAlgorithm.DSA: DSASignature,
                PubKeyAlgorithm.ECDSA: ECDSASignature, }

        if self.pubalg in sigs:
            self.signature = sigs[self.pubalg]()
```

The key provided above specifies the pubkey algorithm as 0x16, which is defined as `PubKeyAlgorithm.EdDSA` but no signature field object is instantiated. So two things should happen:

1) PubKeyAlgorithm.EdDSA should be added to that dict (and EdDSASignature may also need to be defined if the format differs from ECDSASignature) so that it can actually parse

2) an OpaqueSignature class should be added to pgpy/packet/fields.py and used as a fallback, there's no reason for PGPy to raise an exception here.

I plan to implement this tonight and add appropriate test material to the test suite, and then get this and #217 out the door as PGPy v0.4.4

@aidantwoods Sorry for the late reply, I've been looking into if there was a way to fix your issue. Unfortunately you seem to actually have locked yourself out from the OTP part of the YubiKey, and I'd suggest you raise a support ticket here: https://www.yubico.com/support/raise-ticket/

This is expected behavior unfortunately. The NEO can't keep state for more than one applet at once, and opening a connection to the device over one of the other transports (OTP or U2F, which the `list` command does) will cause the NEO to report that the smartcard has been ejected, and then reinserted once the device has been idle for a couple of seconds. During that time the smartcard is unavailable.

What we could do is detect this error specifically on a NEO, and wait up to 2-3 seconds and try again, hiding this issue from the user.

`Pipfile.lock` stores a list of acceptable artifact hashes for each pinned dependency, so once you've *done* a lock, surreptitiously replacing packages is difficult. At lock generation time, explicitly opting in to a source in `Pipfile` is saying "I trust this source not to mess with me, and will use TLS to verify that I'm actually talking to this point of origin". (I think there's an issue somewhere discussing the prospect of binding particular packages to particular source repos, although it may be in `pip` or one of the other PyPA repos, rather than here)

Changing the default index URL (or adding an extra index URL) in `pip.conf`, or using the override feature proposed here through a config file or shell profile based mechanism is different: that's saying "I, or some arbitrary process I ran at some point in time with write access to my home directory (such as an sdist's setup.py file), decided to configure my settings to trust this source of packages". And even a signing scheme like PEP 458 isn't a complete defence against those kinds of shenanigans if the public keys used for verification are themselves stored somewhere inside your home directory rather than in a directory that requires elevated privileges to modify.

There are good reasons why organisations with strict security requirements execute builds on locked down servers with only limited access to the internet at large, or otherwise monitor for these kinds of problems at the network level :)

@kmike reason for this change is conversion to UTC in either relative dates or incomplete dates like `2 pm` was _implicit_ and caused confusions for many users. For example, the site you are parsing dates from is in Eastern timezone, you get correct result normalized to UTC when you parse `2 hrs ago`. But when you parse `today at 2 pm`, no conversion happens because dateparser by default assumed all dates to be in UTC, thus, a wrong recording.

You can also rephrase below as
> Now, dateparser consider timezone to be system's default unless specified. e.g.

dateparser no longer make implicit conversion to a **default** timezone.

Having to specify settings will help user be more aware of timezone conversions and implications. When you specify `TO_TIMEZONE` you know, you're converting from your environment's timezone to the specified one.

re better design, not really sure, how useful is the idea of mixing naive and tzaware objects when you can do general post-processing/normalizing using `settings` to either return all tzaware objects or naive objects converted to a desired timezone.

@pgnassle dateparser offers all of its primary functionality through one entry point `dateparser.parse`. That's why we introduced `settings` to allow customization of factors to _tailor_ the end result. For example, while parsing incomplete date like `Friday`, we can specify `PREFER_DATES_FROM` to either get Friday's date from the last week or next. If day is missing from a date string like `September 2016`, there's a setting to specify use either first of the month or last day to populate the missing day. Timezone conversion settings share the same idea and that's why are consistent with library's design.

For timezones with similar abbreviations, some work was done early on to adopt only the most commonly used ones and avoid duplicates. [See here](https://github.com/scrapinghub/dateparser/blob/master/dateparser/timezones.py)

Not wanting to patch ldaptor (although that would probably be a better approach), I patched ldapserver.py's `_onSearchGotBase` callback

This is what I came up with:

```python
def _onSearchGotBase(...):
    def _sendEntry(...):
        ...
    def _handleAlias(entry, aliasedObjectNames):
            aliasedObjectName, = aliasedObjectNames
            root = interfaces.IConnectedLDAPEntry(self.factory)
            deferred = root.lookup(aliasedObjectName)

            def _handleAliasResolve(newBase):
                deferred = newBase.search(filterObject=request.filter,
                                          attributes=request.attributes,
                                          scope=request.scope,
                                          derefAliases=request.derefAliases,
                                          sizeLimit=request.sizeLimit,
                                          timeLimit=request.timeLimit,
                                          typesOnly=request.typesOnly)
                deferred.addCallback(_handleEntries)
                return deferred

            deferred.addCallback(_handleAliasResolve)
            return deferred

    def _handleEntries(entries):
        pendingAliasResolutions = []
        for entry in entries:
            aliasedObjectName = entry.get('aliasedObjectName')
            if (aliasedObjectName is None or request.derefAliases ==
                    pureldap.LDAP_DEREF_neverDerefAliases):
                _sendEntryToClient(entry)
            else:
                deferred = _handleAlias(entry, aliasedObjectName)
                pendingAliasResolutions.append(deferred)
        if pendingAliasResolutions:
            return twisted.internet.defer.DeferredList(
                pendingAliasResolutions)
    deferred = base.search(...)
    deferred.addCallback(_handleEntries)
    # ... implementation unchanged from here
```

This seems to work, but has the problem of copying the `.search(...)` call. It also is entirely recursive, so aliases resolving to aliases should also work, but does not prevent infinite loops in the case of alias looping

In truth, I would expect this sort of logic to be a part of `IConnectedLDAPEntry.search` instead of at `_onSearchGotBase`, would you be interested in a pull request?

`imread` correctly reads the data stored in the file. It does not invert or convert the data. The photometric interpretation tag defines the color space of the data. In this case False values are white and True values black. Metadata stored in image files are not passed around with numpy arrays and `imshow` is not aware of color spaces either AFAIK. I think it is up to the application developer to handle color spaces and other metadata.

Here are some cases:

a. At the moment, I've some issues running ProjectiveTransform from `skimage`, so I'm using OpenCV for it. OpenCV works in `xy` coordinate system, so the typical call looks like this:
```
    points_fact = np.fliplr(points_fact)
    points_ref = np.fliplr(points_ref)
    coords_src = points_fact.astype(np.float32)[None, ...]
    coords_dst = points_ref.astype(np.float32)[None, ...]
    trans = cv2.getPerspectiveTransform(coords_src, coords_dst)
    ...
    points = np.fliplr(points)
    coords = points.astype(np.float32)[None, ...]
    points_out = cv2.perspectiveTransform(coords, trans)
    points_out = points_out[0, ...]
    points_out = np.fliplr(points_out)
```

b. Our own transforms use `xy` convention (see http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/applications/plot_geometric.html#parameter-estimation). And, as long as https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/1789 is not fixed, this is an issue.

c. `cartesian` coordinate system is useful if user is willing to plot a _polygon_, created by one of our functions (is created in `rc`), __without__ an underlying image (if `imshow` is used, the yaxis is automatically flipped). In this case, they should either switch `xlims` explicitly (i.e. (800, 0)), or apply coordinate transformation on the points.

These are just quite simple cases, but I believe a lot of users got stumbled upon them.

> Personally, I would rather cherry-pick and amend all commit messages from occasional developers

Yes, that's been the usual practice in `master`, and even for commits from not-so-occasional developers, because suggesting wording changes and queueing fully-ready branches so that people would rebase them just to amend the commit message, one branch after another so that pending branches would be fast-forward-mergeable, can result in lot of "empty" waiting time.

> I would like to see all regular/core developers/reviewer to take care about commit and commit message kwalitee. 

:+1: This joins my point expressed above on the fact that we pay great attention to commit atomicity and kwalitee, for good reading/bisecting reasons (which is especially useful in our project with high turn-over of developers); so if people pay more attention *also* to commit log message labelling and structuring, it should not be too big of an add-on burden to what we already do anyway...

(And individual developers will be able to use the kwalitee checker to catch any bad structuring upfront, and reviewers/integrators should pay attention to this in any case while adding "Reviewed-by" QA label to people's branches, as part of `invenio-check-branch` like process.)


I think what you want is:

```
test_1: any(str(), int())
```

The include expects a dictionary.

Previously, xpconanfan (Ang Li) wrote…
This is intentional. 1. test fail, teardown pass -> FAIL 2. teat fail, teardown error -> ERROR The "failure" is not "lost" in case 2. The termination signal is still the original failure, the details and stacktrace fields still use the original failure's info. But an error in teardown test *should* change the status of the test result because something unexpected happened in teardown. A test is tallied as FAIL only if an explicit test failure signal is thrown in test body and no other errors occurred in the test. If anything unexpected happen, the status is ERROR.
1) I disagree entirely. See the pytest example above. When a test fails and an error is thrown in teardown a failure and error are BOTH counted: there is a failure result and an EXTRA error. If the details and stacktrace reflect the failure then the result should also. Otherwise this will only lead to confusion as to why a result with information coming from a mode of failure is marked as error. 2) The logic implemented here itself is inconsistent with your claim. It is possible to call test_fail with no error message: `def test_fail(self, e=None)` and in this scenario the code explicitly subverts the result marked by the user. to replace it with an 'extra_error'. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/284)*

The current design indeed tries to reuse the existing caching mechanism, the advantage is that it makes implementation simpler by reusing existing features. The alternative is to specify a new kind of cache (because no matter what, you must specify in some way that the client must keep the data somewhere, correct me if I'm wrong) with more strict requirements for keeping the data.

If the existing caching mechanism is used, the penalty when something is not properly cached, is that it will be requested by URL instead, so the compression benefit is gone. Ideally web servers would use a unique URL for every different resource that can be used as dictionary.

> So I was proposing to treat the resolution / smoothing in the original
> images via a deconvolution, just like we treat the HST PSF when we take
> input images from HST. Are you saying the smoothing isn't well-defined in
> terms of a scale, and we should just ignore it and enforce some minimum
> size on the target PSF?
>
Correct, this sounds to me like the safest approach.  In the raw, "perfect"
images, there isn't a single well-defined smoothing scale.  We typically
convolve these with a target PSF before using, but I'm guessing you'll want
to choose your own such target.

> Thanks for sending the links that describe the simulated data. Personally,
> I tend to be more interested in something like the "light cone" images, in
> order to simulate a flux-limited sample that has galaxies at a range of
> redshifts. (I guess I should go do some calculations to figure out what is
> the effective flux limit given your 10^10 Msun stellar mass limit, given a
> reasonable range of SEDs, unless you happen to already know this?) Do you
> have an estimated timescale for those to be ready? If it's sometime during
> the summer, I might prefer to wait for those. But if not, then it might be
> worth my setting up code for the z=0 sample just to have some of that
> infrastructure in place and ready to go once the light cones are ready.
>
In the lightcone images, I haven't enforced any stellar mass limit, so the
raw images will have no flux limit, in principle, barring limitations
imposed by the simulation (this will be at L << L* for most relevant
surveys, I believe).  That said, the galaxy populations and properties will
not be a perfect match to reality.  Note that especially the high-z
galaxies are very sensitive to dust modeling, which I have oversimplified
in my existing lightcone images (in the sense that high-z galaxies are too
faint in optical filters, I think).

We might be able to have usable versions (with redshifts tagged) by the end
of June.

@aaronroodman - do you have your own independent calculation of what the PSF should look like with these struts, that I could use as a check on our calculation in GalSim?  The issue I'm having is that I'm used to looking at PSFs with filled in struts, but I don't have great intuition for what the PSF should look like with these spiders.  So yes, there are diffraction spikes (I confirmed convergence of the calculation in terms of sampling) but I would like a way to test that what I'm getting is right.

In case you have time to quickly eyeball some images, I made a few images on a common logarithmic color map (i.e., color scale is the same in both plots, and goes from the peak pixel value in the first image down to 10^-5 of that value).  For both, the pixel scale is 0.02 arcsec, and the image has been convolved with a top hat of that size.  So these are images of the LSST PSF at the field center, without atmosphere or aberrations, with teeny pixels.

First, with LSST aperture size and obscuration, and four *filled-in* struts (with about the right size to be the LSST spider if you filled in the regions between):
![spider_3](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454906/20075065/c1732446-a500-11e6-8aa4-49d0b26fda4a.png)

Second, with the FITS image you gave me of the pupil plane, so including the spider properly in principle (assuming that all the sampling is done right etc.):
![spider_2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454906/20075086/d0183d38-a500-11e6-8389-f3fc46b14f3f.png)

We can see that the diffraction features change in two ways:
(1) In the first case, there is some area around the diffraction spikes that is sort of blocked out, whereas in the second case, there is not.

(2) The location of the bumps in the diffraction spikes changes.

You can see some low-level numerical issues where the results go slightly negative and that gives the little boxes out near the array edge in the second case.  If I increase the level of oversampling in GalSim, the resulting PSF does not change, just this low-level numerical noise moves around.  So it seems like the calculation has converged.

Finally, use of the pupil plane image changes the image rendering time (compared to the filled-in struts case) by a factor of 40.  This is without me making any attempt at speeding anything up.

@k1-hedayati you mind opening a PR with that role and rolebinding to the k8s template, I must admit I've been struggling with this problem (failed reading pods, and silent endpoint updates) myself as well.

Possible sources of race conditions:

**Race Conditions During Failover:**

We have a number of potential race conditions during failover, due to the fact that we have three actions which need to be peformed (master lock, promotion, endpoint update) and no way to perform them in a "transaction".  No matter how many master lock checks we perform, there is always the possibility of the pending master's container being suspended between two lines of code.  The primary danger areas are:

1. new master is suspended between the master lock check and promoting itself.
2. new master is suspended between performing the failover and updating the endpoint

In either case, we can get a period of inconsistency during which another master has promoted itself due to the first pending master losing the lock, but proceeding to promote and/or update the endpoint anyway.  During this period, data writes could go to the wrong master and be lost.

Proposal:  Add multiple master lock checks in order to minimize period of inconsistency, e.g.

1. get master lock
2. promote
3. check master lock, if lost attempt to find correct master, and become replica of it, otherwise shut down
4. set endpoint
5. check master lock, if lost set endpoint to null and reboot as read-only

Level 3 radial files contain a PPI scan from a single elevation scan for a particular moment or product (rain rate, etc).  Multiple moments from the same radar and time period could be combined with the `add_field` method of the **Radar** class.  Combining multiple sweeps is more tricky.  When fixed/re-designed the `join_radar` function (PR #127) could be used to make Radar objects composable so that multiple level 3 files could be used to build up an entire radar volume from the individual sweeps and moments.  Implemented **Sweep** and **Ray** classes (PR #242) could make process more user friendly.  

This method requires a lot of work to generate a more complete but still not comprehensive NEXRAD volume.  A Level 2 file provides the complete volume in a single file, I think directing users at these files when this level of information is needed is more prudent.  Level 2 files do not provide KDP, rain rates and a few other higher order products  which Level 3 file provide.  Also the moments in Level 2 data are not corrected (velocity dealiasing, etc)  where as the Level 3 data is so there still cases where Level 3 data provides helpful information.

I think the most generic approach here would be to utilize the `pyproj` package to compute the locations of each (x, y) point in a `Grid`. The idea here is that the user would be able to more accurately compute these locations  anywhere on the Earth through the ability to change the `proj` kwarg for the map projection, and to a lesser extent the `datum` and `ellps` kwargs. Just my two cents.

相同与不同，主要是我们看这个问题的出发点不一样。
从一个计算机专业人员的角度看，当然这是没有不同的，如你所说，窃得哈希值一样能连接服务器，或者说我也可以直接在设置时先哈希一次然后人工输入哈希值作为密钥以避免明文密码的问题。
但是，使用Shadowsocks的用户群不见得都是有CS相关知识的，潜在的威胁主要来自：
1.用户可能使用与其他网站相同的密码，明文存储在本地可能导致攻击者不仅仅拥有的是连接服务器的权限。加密连接可能compromise事小，但可能的结果远不止此；
2.现在有很多用户，他们并非自己搭建的VPS，而是购买的服务提供商的服务。在此情况下，这个加密模型强制服务提供商必须知道该密码明文，建立账户时必须传送该密码明文。如果服务提供商本身不可信，那么该密码可能被用于其他不为人知的用途。如果注册账户的网页没有采用加密的https连接，那么中间人也可能窃取该密码明文；

As you said, it's the same. The crypto-model does NOT change, sure. But that's from a perspective of the professional. For normal users, they have little to no knowledge of computer science, nor cryptography. The potential threat is not the security of the encrypted connection, it is the password itself. Many users use the same password for their other online account (fact), by exposing this password, you are not only compromising the security of a connection or a server. You are giving those attackers a shortcut. Yes, my suggestion has no difference than setting the hash result as password, but normal users wouldn't know this method.
As for my opinion, when designing a software, you should never ever store sensitive user input (such as password) in plaintext. Either you encrypt it, which is obviously not possible, since Shadowsocks is an open-source project, you can't have a private key to encrypt the file. Or you use the hash instead, which is my previous post suggesting.
I hope you understand the gravity of this request.

Just now (pex 1.1.17), I'm trying to run `pex` with `--no-index --find-links=$my_wheelhouse`. I can confirm that all my needed dependencies, including wheel 0.29.0 and several acceptable versions of setuptools, are pure-python `.whl` files in the `$my_wheelhouse` directory.

The pex command fails, and when `-vvvvv` used, here's the output:

```
pex -vvvvv --python=/usr/local/python3.4/bin/python3.4 --python-shebang=/usr/local/python3.4/bin/python3.4 --no-index --find-links=/storage/mystuff/wheels mypkg -o /storage/mystuff/pex/mypkg-1482339345.pex
pex: Building pex
pex: Building pex :: Resolving interpreter
pex: Building pex :: Resolving interpreter :: Setting up interpreter /usr/local/python3.4/bin/python3.4
pex: Constructed RequestsContext context <pex.http.RequestsContext object at 0x7f55dd6a17f0>
pex: crawling link i=0 link=Link('file:///storage/mystuff/wheels') follow_links=False
pex: Building pex :: Resolving interpreter :: Setting up interpreter /usr/local/python3.4/bin/python3.4 :: Interpreter cache resolving wheel<0.30.0,>=0.26.0
Could not find compatible interpreter

(received nonzero return code 102 while executing!)
```

So I think the problem is that neither `wheel` nor `setuptools` can be installed from wheels during the interpreter bootstrapping; [instead a SourcePackage is required](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/fa3bfc20155c015fc991f0dac0f64d23ebaf6f35/pex/bin/pex.py#L385). This makes sense, since the bootstrapper can't rely on wheel being present.
 
If I instead fetch `wheel` and `setuptools` source packages via `pip download` and place these source packages in `$my_wheelhouse`, then `pex --no-index --find-links=$my_wheelhouse ...` succeeds in bootstrapping the environment.

My questions:
- Is there a good place to document this restriction on interpreter bootstrapping?
- Is there a good way to inform the user at runtime, when wheel or setuptools can't be bootstrapped and when --no-index and --find-links are passed options, that source packages for each need to be located in the find-links directory?

There are a few pre-existing issues to address to support this:

  Here only one of the junit xml result files for `#testEmpty` or `#testAddFile` will win. We should produce both files in unique locations and merge their results.

+ Code coverage results are not emitted in a way that they are later mergeable.
  
  If we run in per-target mode, we'll want to merge coverage results across all the individual test target runs.

> I'm going to base my work on the ChangeCalculator and TargetRootsCalculator implementations.
The difference is that while ChangeCalculator has to calculate which files were changed, we already have the files from the --owner-of arguments.

Yep! Which I think means that you won't need to touch `ChangeCalculator`... only `TargetRootsCalculator` and friends.

> Finally, the --owner-of option will also be based on the implementation of --changed, with the key differences that --owner-of can be repeated.

Regarding where the options are defined: because `--changed-*` is actually a whole family of options, those options are colocated on a "Subsystem" (ie: a collection of options).

For `--owner-of`, rather than a `Subsystem`, I think that you should put the option directly in the "bootstrap options": https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/3ae1a086e228cd7fdf4f3e9f1e993cfd47bb95c4/src/python/pants/option/global_options.py#L69-L251

> On another note, how should pants behave when both --changed and --owner-of are specified?

It should fail with a good error message: here is what we currently do when literal specs and `--changed` options are specified: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/3ae1a086e228cd7fdf4f3e9f1e993cfd47bb95c4/src/python/pants/init/target_roots_calculator.py#L60-L62

----

Finally, as to testing: I'd suggest starting out by just testing that the option works as expected by doing things like:
```
# hypothetical
$ ./pants --owner-of=src/scala/org/pantsbuild/zinc/analysis/AnalysisMap.scala list
src/scala/org/pantsbuild/zinc/analysis:analysis
```
...which should always be ~equivalent to the output of the existing `list-owners` goal:
```
$ ./pants list-owners -- src/scala/org/pantsbuild/zinc/analysis/AnalysisMap.scala
src/scala/org/pantsbuild/zinc/analysis:analysis
```

Then, add an integration test following a model like: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/3ae1a086e228cd7fdf4f3e9f1e993cfd47bb95c4/tests/python/pants_test/engine/legacy/test_list_integration.py#L31-L38 ... which you could even run using your new option! Meta.

----

I am not sure. Inlining swagger file has some limitations:
* Customers might run into their CloudFormation template size limitations
* You would include CloudFormation constructs (like intrinsics or resource names) in your Swagger file, which makes it harder to share with others

Instead, it would be better to extend `aws cloudformation package` command to support S3 upload functionality for AWS::Include property. Customers can then choose to use the Include style if they want.

I don't use this script anymore but was having my own race and other verification problems so checked in to see how this script handles them.

Personally I believe that this PR doesn't actually solve anything.

#### AllowReassociation=False does work

The Boto source is the way it is because the AWS API defaults to false. If two instances race for the same Elastic IP and reassociation isn't allowed then a `ClientError` with a code of `Resource.AlreadyAssociated` will be raised.

Thus because this script doesn't allow reassociations (#13) there is no benefit to verifying with `get_all_addresses` / `describe_addresses` as done in this PR.

#### An association is not a remap

This is the real problem (for me at least): just because you've successfully associated with an Elastic IP doesn't mean it has been remapped and is your public IP yet.

You can verify this with the following code:

```
import boto3
import requests
```

Autobahn follows these rules (in it's cross network framework parts):

* only plain Deferreds/Futures, no co-routines
* everything managed via `txaio.create_future` etc
* using `six` and strict bytes/unicode (`b"..` vs `u".."`)

And thus can support anything from Python 2.7 upwards with both Twisted and asyncio (the latter being polyfilled for Python <3.3).

Crossbar.io has these rules:

* Twisted only
* Python 2.7 is supported, and hence no await/async, but Twisted inlineCallbacks

> We do want Twisted here because it has a much broader scope than asyncio, and includes support for a lot things already. So requiring Python 3.5+ would bring as a minor syntactical win. I have no idea about performance if using await/async vs the Twisted "hack" of using exceptions for co-routines (which is ingenius really). But PyPy isn't at 3.5 anyway - and this is a killer.

Found it. Some more detailed steps:

* open a complete source file
* open navigation pane if necessary
* collapse working-storage in navigation panel

Result: Working storage is collapsed in code panel, too. (One can argue if this is what the programmer wants [especially if the navigation panel is locked], but it looks like a feature-by-design).

Now doing the same again with a file which is NOT complete (working-storage assumed):

I think I know what happened:

* open a source file without headers ("starting" with a variable definition)
* open navigation pane if necessary
* collapse working-storage in navigation panel (as there is no working-storage in the code it cannot be collapsed in code view)
* click somewhere in the code panel (variable definitions)

Result: Navigation panel unfolds working-storage "as the cursor is in there" --> likely not intended (neither by the user not by you)


And the last one which was the initial issue:

* open a source file without headers ("starting" with a variable definition)
* open navigation pane if necessary
* collapse working-storage in navigation panel (as there is no working-storage in the code it cannot be collapsed in code view)
* click somewhere in the code panel (somewhere in the procedure division) --> nothing happens with navigation panel as we're in the procedure division which is already completely unfolded --> fine
* press any key (= change something in the code pane, either directly or via paste/cut)

Result: Navigation panel unfolds working-storage definitely not intended


This lead to another observation:

* open a complete source file
* open navigation pane if necessary
* collapse a sub-item in working-storage in navigation panel
* collapse working-storage in navigation panel
* un-collapse working-storage in navigation panel

Result: the sub-item in working-storage is still collapsed --> fine

Now with assumed headers:

* open a source file without headers ("starting" with a variable definition)
* open navigation pane if necessary
* collapse a sub-item in working-storage in navigation panel
* collapse working-storage in navigation panel
* un-collapse working-storage in navigation panel

Result: Every sub-item of working-storage in navigation panel is unfolded


My suggestion is to don't unfold anything in the navigation pane "automatically" if navigation pane is locked [this should give a possible workaround for the bug part, too], if possible: please provide this in OCIDE 4.7.3.

Not sure how hard it is possible to fix the bug in navigation panel with assumed headers. Does this have to wait for pycode 3.0?

The same bug I mentioned above showed up again, one job not finishing. The pool acted like it had no work to assign but it had not exited and I was at 577/578 completion. Earlier I'd noticed one of the worker processes was stalled (you can see this in `htop`; the name of the process never changes to a job source name). I gave that idle worker a SIGTERM and the pool spawned off a new job, but I bet whatever job had been assigned to it got lost forever. @jhamrick The comment I made was about getting the nbgrader_config.py correct for the student. If they have an error, then they (of course) don't see any error. The errors can happen in two main ways:

1. The file doesn't get properly parsed (syntax error). I have a system-wide nbgrader_config.py, so the result is that students see the wrong course_id assignments (because it falls back to that config).
2. The file parses, but has an incorrect property or value, like wrong course_id. This often results in no assignments showing in the Assignments List tab.

I am leaning toward having a system-level config that maps students to a course_id list. That would make this issue go away.

Even if we try to adhere to pep-8, we don't enforce it, but more especially the pep says:

> However, know when to be inconsistent -- sometimes style guide recommendations just aren't applicable. When in doubt, use your best judgment. Look at other examples and decide what looks best. And don't hesitate to ask!

So we will likely never *enforce* it. 

Regardless of enforcing, a gigantic pull request that just fixes all pep-8 will  be more problematic than helpful in my experience. It will break the ability to work with tools like git-bisect and git-blame, and can make things complex to backport. That's from experience, as I did this mistakes a couple of years in the past, and still regularly hate myself for having done so. So we usually do not accept wide range code base cleanup. 

One helpful things though would be instead of enforcing pep8 on the all codebase enforce it on the `diff`. Tools like pep8-radius can do that IIRC. This allow the code to progressively become compliant, and still give the maintainers the ability to overwrite the tool decision without having test to permanently fail. 

I strongly recommend [Beyond pep8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wf-BqAjZb8M) if you have 1h to spare (or even in the background).

I've recently setup yapf (instead of autopep8) in my editor to only apply to lines I have changed (it's broken right now I should fix this). And one of my wishlist is a bot you could mention on a PR to autopep8 only _part_ of a file (things that have changed only, and only if requested).

In the end I think that having at least pep8 as a linter and  progressively go toward no-errors will be helpful to catch potential mistakes (like `local variable 'app' is assigned to but never used`), but I still think a large change like this one is too disruptive. 

That's just my though, and I'll let other dev pitchin in.  and I have to say no to things like that as I know it can be quite the effort to work through. 

Does that make some sense to you or do you disagree ?

In your tests use the `tempfile`package to get a temporary directory. In `jenkins.sh` set `TMPDIR` to point at the ephemeral volume. Just copy the relevant portions from Toil's jenkins.sh.

Also, correct me if I'm wrong, but I don't believe running the entire toil pipeline within a singularity container would also be within the same singularity "vm" on other machines that are provisioned by toil to run the toil jobs.

The interface would have been much simpler/cleaner if the latest release of singularity supported pulling docker containers from quay.io repos, but their parser doesn't quite work for those urls yet. It turns out singularity can pull and run docker containers themselves, but they can only be pulled from docker-hub right now and singularity-hub. I can only put one docker container on docker-hub for free, and I'm running into some problems compiling singularity images from scratch on singularity-hub. So as a workaround until singularity supports pulling docker containers from quay.io I'm going to simply have toil-vg run singularity from locally stored singularity images on a shared filesystem (aka Biowulf). I was hoping to keep this simple, but all of this stuff got in my way.

It's a long shot, but it can happen. Deleting the venv would be a good thing to do if we detect that something's gone wrong.

When I deploy things to a persistent server, I generally start a fresh venv. Otherwise (and this is the case for letsencrypt, too), old, no-longer-needed dependencies never get removed. Because of Python's mutability and dynamicity, it's conceivable that those dependencies could get themselves called and alter the state of the interpreter in unfriendly ways (like my evil library [tracefront](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tracefront/)). More prosaically, they could register setuptools entrypoints that should no longer be there.

I don't know enough details about your setup to know exactly what the issue is, but when you say, "I'm having to create this on a separate server that doesn't have the SSL virtualhost for heimkoma.com", it sounds like you're doing something that would produce this error!

Hi, I started looking at this a bit.

asyncio has the ability to watch file descriptors for read availability (https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-eventloop.html#watch-file-descriptors)

librdkafka can write to a file descriptor when a queue is readable with `rd_kafka_queue_io_event_enable()` (https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/blob/7478b5ef16aadd6543fe38bc6a2deb895c70da98/src/rdkafka.h#L2362)

Would it be possible to expose `rd_kafka_queue_io_event_enable()` in the python API? I don't believe the queues are exposed currently, so confluent-kafka-python would have to attach the appropriate internal queue itself.

Ideally you should be able to pass a file descriptor and a payload. When the file descriptor becomes readable, call `poll()` or `consume()`.

There are still a handful of synchronous operations (like `commit()`, `committed()`, etc.) but consuming and producing messages is still the most heavily used operation.

asyncio support could probably be integrated more directly in this library, but the above might be a good starting point to allow for an asycio wrapper.
Oh, flush() will return as soon as the output queue length reaches 0 (or timeout occurs)

`required=True` will make your field invalid if the input is *missing*. `None` is considered an input, so 

```python
MySchema().load({'required_field': None})
```

will validate.

If you do not want to allow `None`, set `allow_none=False` on the field.

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class MySchema(Schema):
    required_field = fields.Field(allow_none=False, required=False)
schema = MySchema()
print(schema.validate({'required_field': None}))
# {'required_field': ['Field may not be null.']}
```
Looks like polymorphism. You might be interested in https://github.com/maximkulkin/marshmallow-oneofschema.

@frol It's not clear that these class Meta options (`required`, `dump_only`...) belong in marshmallow core, since they are only relevant for libraries that auto-generate fields. This has been pointed out [here](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/453#issuecomment-280951726). So for now we won't add `required` as a class Meta option and leave it up to consuming libraries to add it.

The problem is that `.timetuple()` only works with second-level precision. To keep the microsecond precision, use `.timestamp()`.

```python
>>> s = '2011-09-28 12:26:18.089952593'
>>> t = datetime.datetime.strptime(s[:-4], "%Y-%m-%d %I:%M:%S.%f")
>>> t
datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 28, 0, 26, 18, 89950)
>>> t.timetuple()
time.struct_time(tm_year=2011, tm_mon=9, tm_mday=28, tm_hour=0, tm_min=26, tm_sec=18, tm_wday=2, tm_yday=271, tm_isdst=-1)
>>> time.mktime(t.timetuple())
1317183978.0
>>> t.timestamp()
1317183978.08995
```

If you're asking what I think you're asking, then yes. To get the value of memory and registers at a given point, you need to look at the state right after that code was executed.

It's possible the solution you're looking for is [https://github.com/angr/claripy/blob/master/claripy/annotation.py](annotations!) Annotations are, effectively, a customizable tainting interface that let you add whatever extra data you want to any value in angr. There's also a (more complicated) interface that lets you query ASTs for any properties you like, including any custom logic you could desire for mixing annotations - (https://github.com/angr/claripy/blob/master/claripy/backends/__init__.py)[claripy backends]. Look at (this simple type analysis backend)[https://github.com/angr/simuvex/blob/master/simuvex/s_type_backend.py] for a good example for how to use backends and annotations to intelligently propagate taints with semantic information.

The changes look fine to me. The only wrinkle I can think of is emptying an existing collection, but you already thought of that by handling tombstones.

I'm not 100% sure how to handle updating timestamps that are in the past. If we set last_modified back to the "new" old record, then we will re-sync certain records. If we don't update last_modified, there's no way for the client to know about the records in the past. I think we have to accept that putting records in the past is going to break synchronization. Based on that, I would say we should try to keep last_modified monotonic.

My opinion, therefore, about the right behavior of the two tests:

- `test_create_ignores_specified_last_modified_if_in_the_past`: `timestamp` should be the same as `timestamp_before` (change `assertGreater` to `assertEqual`).
- `test_update_ignores_specified_last_modified_if_in_the_past`: `timestamp` goes backwards here because the `last_modified` of the only record goes backwards. We could handle this by insert into `timestamps` on the first record save, which will ensure that the collection's timestamp doesn't go backwards in this case, but still leaves us open to slightly more complicated cases (record A is inserted, followed by record B; timestamp_before is taken; B is put 10 seconds in the past; timestamp is now record A). So I think the right behavior here is to do nothing (let `timestamp` go backwards). Anyone explicitly setting last_modified had better know what they're doing and might break synchronization.

Proposal:

```ini
kinto.background.processes = 4
```

When I started working on this issue I had 4 ideas how to implement it:
1. Create `TimerUs` as subclass of `Timer` and add `mTimerType` field to determine if given timer in the queue is millisecond or microsecond precision. This approach adds 4 bytes to every `Timer` instance and 8 bytes to every `TimerUs` instance. It allows to optimize comparing of `Timer` instances (cost would be a lot of conditions to select proper compare function) while comparing of `TimerUs` or `Timer` with `TimerUs` would not be optimal. This approach creates relatively small FLASH overhead.
2. Add microsecond precision to the `Timer` class. It adds 4 bytes to every instance. Optimization of millisecond timer would be hard in this case. The code would be clean and would produce relatively small image.
3. Create `TimerUs` class as subclass of `Timer` and distinguish them by adding to separate queues. It does not increase size of `Timer` class and `TimerUs` is 4 bytes larger than `Timer`. To reuse similar code for both queues I used template approach. It allows optimization for `Timer` comparison (probably the fastest approach) but produces a lot of code in flash due to 2 specializations of 4 templates.
4. Create `TimerUs` as subclass of `Timer` and use virtual methods to distinguish types. It increases `Timer` class and `TimerUs` and provides vtables for those classes but it would allow to write clean and easy to maintain code.

I used the 3rd approach to preserve as much RAM as possible (to fit in CC2538 chip) and to allow as fast operations as possible (to preserve battery power). I'm open to discussion which approach should we use. IMHO 2 would be better than 1 because it uses less RAM and the code would be easy to maintain.

Today I came with one more idea: instead of using mT0 and mDt combination we could use 64-bit mTriggerTime for the `Timer` class. It would provide enough precision for every timer and there would be no problem with overflows (first one in a few hundred years). Also size of `Timer` class would not change. Computations on 64-bit are not as optimal as 32 but much better than on structure containing both milliseconds and microseconds. It seems to be good alternative for the timer design.

Mailgun's approach of [merging "custom variables"](http://mailgun-documentation.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user_manual.html#attaching-data-to-messages) directly into the posted event is really... inconvenient. Among other issues, it means you must avoid choosing custom variable names that could conflict with any current *or future* Mailgun tracking event properties if you want to access those variables from a webhook.

I'm starting to think the only way for Anymail to (semi-)reliably recover metadata (custom user-variables) from a Mailgun tracking event is to just include *all* event data that doesn't match a known Mailgun property. It'll be a little fragile -- if/when Mailgun adds new event properties, Anymail will think those are metadata until it's updated. I'll look at making that change.

A useful Mailgun feature request would be for them to include the 'user-variables' event property in the fields posted to tracking webhooks. (user-variables is available through their [events API](http://mailgun-documentation.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api-events.html#event-structure); it's just not posted to the webhooks in un-merged form.)

Since I am making an explicit copy, I let go of this restriction and don't return false even if the matrix is [not writable](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/1152/files#diff-52f1945d779be1e60903590907bb9326R542) if the array is of non-POD type.  [Quoting from their docs](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/cast/eigen.html):

>When a bound function parameter is instead Eigen::Ref<MatrixType> (note the lack of const), pybind11 will only allow the function to be called if it can be mapped and if the numpy array is writeable (that is a.flags.writeable is true). Any access (including modification) made to the passed variable will be transparently carried out directly on the numpy.ndarray.

So, if I'm passing a numpy matrix of `AutoDiffScalar`s with `writeable = False` from python and I have `Ref<Matrix<AutoDiffScalar>>` in the corresponding C++, with my change it will work. Because of [this](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/1152/files#diff-52f1945d779be1e60903590907bb9326R542).  However, if a numpy matrix of doubles with `writeable=False` is passed to `Ref<MatrixXd>` it won't work because of the documented behavior. From #2129

> [...] This, however, doesn't fix the case where a node acc1 which has ether opens a channel with a node acc2 which doesn't have ether.

Raiden can handle the `acc2` case describe above better, by having a counter of how many channel lifecycles the node's ether support. In the case above that would be `0`, and if a new channel is opened with `acc2` the channel must stay in an inactive state, this is quite important because `acc2` shouldn't accept off-chain payments which it cannot withdraw on-chain.

Note: The above is required if we don't trust/rely on MSs. If we realy on MSs to call update the account doesn't need any ether, since the MSs can call update transfer on the user's behalf and the user can pay the MS off-chain.
