Currently PIL has very little support for scientific imaging, i.e. `Gaussian_Blur` on a `float32` image, or handling an image with several `float32` color channels. I'm not sure what the demand is for these features, but I would be happy to contribute any of my implementations to Torchvision.

The challenge here is that we do want to allow for things like custom supervisors, where code inside the nursery *takes over* for `__aexit__`. For example, see `trio._core._run.Runner.init`, which is the actual root of trio's task tree. It's nursery supervises. the user's main task + system tasks, and it has a special bit of semantics: in addition to the normal rule that it cancels everything when any task crashes, it also cancels everything when the user's main task exits successfully.

But, it just occurred to me that because implementing a custom supervisor is a pretty unusual thing to be doing, we might be able to define some heuristic that catches most accidental misuses, and then if you intentionally want to block inside the nursery then there's a special flag you can enable. Like maybe it would make sense to have `open_nursery` *by default* raise an error if the body tries to execute a checkpoint, but you can do `open_nursery(allow_checkpoints=True)` to allow it.

I guess the question then is whether "contains a checkpoint" is a good heuristic for "something is wrong"... thinking out loud here, the cases we want to catch are when someone starts doing arbitrary amounts of work directly inside the nursery. That's almost never the right thing to do because "parenting is a full time job". In trio, if you're doing arbitrary amounts of work then you pretty much have to execute some checkpoints in the process. It's possible to avoid this (e.g. `time.sleep(10000)`), but there's not much trio can do about this in general (it literally has no control while the `sleep` is running), it's a much more general problem than just nurseries, and people are relatively unlikely to do this by accident. So "no checkpoints" does probably rule out the cases we want to rule out.

Are there any *good* reasons to block inside a nursery scope (i.e., does this heuristic have false positives)? There are several tests in trio's test suite that do it, but they're a bit sloppy and mostly just do things like `wait_all_tasks_blocked` to make sure one child has blocked before spawning the next. I think I've been moving away from this over time too (moving the `wait_all_tasks_blocked` into the individual children instead of doing it in the parent). And I wouldn't mind having to pass an extra kwarg to `open_nursery` in the occasional test that wants to be sloppy. So... I think probably this is a reasonable heuristic?

(Note: there's another way of implementing the above idea that's equivalent except for cosmetics: make `open_nursery` a low-level API that most people don't use, and have the ordinary version be something like `async with trio.multi_spawn() as spawner:`, where `multi_spawn` enforces the no-checkpoint rule. Sort of like the distinction between `open_cancel_scope` vs `move_on_after`-and-friends. But I think I'm not a big fan; the "nursery" name is relatively evocative, and you need to use and pass around the object in very common situations. I guess it could be `open_nursery()` and `open_supervisor_nursery()` or something, if we want to follow Guido's heuristic that if a function has a bool argument and its callers always pass a constant, then it should be a separate function.)

My feeling for right now is that maybe it's best to wait a bit before making any decisions, since we're still learning about how we want to use `open_nursery` and about how much trouble newcomers have with this. But it does seem plausible that we could do something here and maybe should.

For the autodetection thing, I'd suggest picking of these conventions:

* `map_sync` takes a sync fn, `map_async` takes an async fn

* `map` takes a sync fn, `map_async` takes an async fn

* `map_sync` takes a sync fn, `map` takes an async fn

(The other option is `amap`, but that convention mostly gets used when the function being named is itself async, so I wouldn't use it here – you don't want people to think they should be writing `await stream.map(...)`.)

Trio itself uses the last convention; for example, [`BlockingTrioPortal`](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-core.html#trio.BlockingTrioPortal) has `run_sync` and `run` methods. But that's for trio, where it's fair to assume that people are usually interested in doing async things :-). For your trio-ized version of streamz, you might want to do the same, or keep the sync versions undecorated for consistency with the regular version of streamz, or make everything explicit ... your call really.

Anyway, sounds like the issue in the title is resolved, so I'm going to close this. But I'm happy to keep chatting here or [in the chat](https://gitter.im/python-trio/general)!

Hi, I took another look at the code today and it turns out that manual padding for average pooling op is not a good idea. Sorry for prematurely attributing this error to the TF issue. 

As you may know Tensorflow does not support fine-grained specification of padding. For a quick example, check out here https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/nn/avg_pool. This is giving us a lot of trouble.  In our latest attempt to battle this issue, we special-cased some scenarios where native pooling ops can be used and implemented our own python version of pooling as a custom TF node which mimics Caffe2 behavior. And we are very excited to see that doing so helped us pass all 9 model tests on both CPU and GPU (interestingly, TF supports different tensor formats depending on whether you are on a CPU or a GPU thus the behavior of our converter is different on each device).

As for moving under a onnx repo, I brought up this idea to our team during today's meeting and we are very excited to do so. Would you like me to push our repo here once we clear all the backend tests (I will integrate these tests very soon)?

> the default value when setting this up inside vault and leaving out the sts_endpoint is sts.amazonaws.com

Yes

> that has an implied region of us-east-1?

I believe so, from my testing.

> Maybe hvac or even vault should return something a bit more human parsable

Yeah, the tricky thing there is that, to do this, Vault would need to look at the Authorization header, parse the Credential scope out of that, parse the region out of the credential scope, and then try to infer a desired region out of the STS endpoint. It would also preclude using a custom STS endpoint, or it would just have to assume the region is correct when using a custom STS endpoint. Another option might be to compute the right STS endpoint based on the region in the request, if the endpoint wasn't overridden, though there's still the open question of, when using the us-east-1 region, do you use the global STS endpoint or the regional us-east-1 STS endpoint, or is there any difference between the two?

Anyway, if you want to continue this discussion about what Vault should do, we should move it to the Vault issue tracker (it's only by coincidence that I happened to see this here).
